response:
## Business brief

**Mode:** create | **Domain:** OR | **Eligible:** yes

A dispatcher must release vehicle routes and job start times within a five-minute computation budget, using known jobs, travel times, capacities, and time windows. Search ordering matters because the executable plan is the best feasible incumbent available when computation stops. The research problem is whether the proposed algorithm yields lower routing-and-lateness cost than a reference method by minute five on relevant instances—not whether it is universally faster.

**DFC-12:** Actor—dispatcher. Trigger—a dispatch plan must be released within five minutes. Choices—routes and job start times. Decision-time information—jobs, travel times, capacities, and time windows. Stakes—routing plus lateness cost of the released incumbent. Frictions—vehicle capacity, time windows, and the five-minute budget. Mechanism—search ordering changes when high-quality feasible incumbents appear. Trade-off—solution improvement versus decision latency. Counterfactual—the reference method under identical hardware, instances, and time limit. Model mapping—routes/start times are decisions; the five-minute limit is a computational constraint; incumbent cost is the relevant outcome. Evidence status—frequent early quality is a hypothesis until comparative evidence is supplied. Boundaries—no untested or all-instance performance claim.

**Consistency gates:** Actor—pass. Timing—pass, only the minute-five incumbent can be released. Information—pass. Behavior—pass, value flows through incumbent quality at cutoff. Constraints—pass. Objective—pass, routing and lateness cost are preserved.

**Model mapping:** `vehicle routes/job start times` → decisions; `capacity/time windows` → constraints; `search ordering` → algorithmic mechanism; `incumbent at five minutes` → outcome; `runtime behavior across instances` → unresolved computational uncertainty.

**Claims:** [model assumption] five minutes is the operative computation budget. [unsupported claim] the algorithm produces better incumbents within five minutes; test against the named reference on representative instances with common hardware, solver settings, seeds where relevant, and statistical uncertainty. [unsupported claim] superiority on all instances; remove.

**Boundary conditions:** operational value is limited to the plan available at cutoff; no statistical superiority has yet been established; faster search does not itself establish realized service or profit effects. **Primary remaining weakness:** the comparative five-minute performance evidence is missing.

**Mode result — business problem:** Compare the objective value and feasibility of incumbents produced by the proposed and reference algorithms at the five-minute dispatch deadline, and identify instance conditions under which earlier search progress changes the executable plan.
